title: Localization and Thermalization in Highly-Excited Many-Body Quantum Systems
speaker: David Huse
affiliation: Princeton University; Member, School of Mathematics

proof_stages.label: Historical motivation for finite-temperature many-body localization
proof_stages.summary: The speaker frames many-body localization at positive or high temperature as the problem Anderson originally targeted and as revived by Basko, Aleiner, and Altshuler, emphasizing the possibility of zero dc conductivity in an interacting isolated system.

proof_stages.label: Random local quantum-spin Hamiltonian setup
proof_stages.summary: After recalling localized single-particle eigenstates, the speaker defines random short-range spin/qubit Hamiltonians on a tensor-product Hilbert space, gives concrete one-dimensional variants, and stresses that the system is isolated rather than coupled to an external bath.

proof_stages.label: Infinite-temperature phase diagram of the random-field chain
proof_stages.summary: For the random-field Heisenberg chain at infinite temperature, all eigenstates are weighted equally and the putative phase diagram has an ergodic/thermal phase at weak disorder, a many-body localized phase above roughly h/J≈3.5, and a special unstable integrable point at zero disorder.

proof_stages.label: Diagnostic dictionary for the two phases
proof_stages.summary: The two phases are characterized by linked diagnostics: ergodic versus nonergodic dynamics, heat-bath versus non-bath behavior, continuous versus discrete local spectra, Wigner-Dyson versus Poisson level statistics, transport versus no dc transport, decoherence versus coherence, and thermal volume-law versus athermal local eigenstates.

proof_stages.label: Eigenstate entanglement formalism
proof_stages.summary: To make the entanglement diagnostic precise, a block Schmidt decomposition and von Neumann entanglement entropy are introduced, with thermal eigenstates expected to have entropy proportional to the block volume.

proof_stages.label: Strong-disorder localized eigenstates and local qubits
proof_stages.summary: Starting from the J=0 product bit-string eigenstates and invoking perturbative stability into the disordered phase, the speaker describes interacting localized eigenstates as deformed local two-level degrees of freedom with exponentially decaying matrix elements and spin correlations.

proof_stages.label: Numerical status and difficulty of the transition
proof_stages.summary: The numerical and conceptual status is assessed: localized eigenstates can be probed by exact diagonalization and low-entanglement methods, but the thermal side and the critical point are hard and are not captured by standard single-particle localization or equilibrium phase-transition theory.

proof_stages.label: Thouless-energy scaling conjecture for many-body criticality
proof_stages.summary: For the transition, the speaker imports the Thouless-energy criterion from single-particle Anderson criticality and conjectures that the many-body critical relaxation scale matches the exponentially small many-body level spacing, giving an infinite dynamic critical exponent.

examples.name: Hard-core lattice particles as two-level systems
examples.note: The occupancy/not-occupancy of a lattice site is used to make the spin-half/qubit abstraction concrete without being needed for the later argument.
examples.engagement_reason: He describes hard-core lattice particles only as an instance of a two-level system, with occupancy ‘there or not there,’ without doing further work on that particle model.

passes.reason: ordinary hard-core occupancy realizes a standard local two-level Hilbert space
Hard-core occupancy leaves only the minimal 0/1 local Hilbert space, used as a standard modeling identification rather than to refute a claim.
Ordinary hard-core lattice occupancy is used as a spin-1/2/two-level representation, not to refute a claim.
hard-core lattice particles with occupancy 0/1 are used as a standard realization of local two-level systems, not as a sporadic or adversarial case
Hard-core occupancy collapses each lattice site to the minimal 0/1 two-state Hilbert space, used only as a modeling equivalence.
Hard-core lattice particles are used as an ordinary realization of local two-state spin/qubit degrees of freedom.
standard hard-core site occupancy gives an ordinary 0/1 qubit encoding, used as a modeling equivalence.
Hard-core occupancy gives a minimal 0/1 local Hilbert space, the infinite-exclusion limit, used only to motivate spin/qubit models.
Ordinary hard-core occupancy is used as a representative realization of local two-level spin/qubit degrees of freedom.
ordinary hard-core occupancy is used as a representative two-level local Hilbert-space model.
Hard-core lattice particles are used as a standard realization of local two-level degrees of freedom, not to refute a claim.
standard hard-core occupancy/absence variables are used as representative two-level systems

examples.name: Low-energy localized tails outside the infinite-temperature statement
examples.note: In response to a question, rare low-energy states on the thermal side are mentioned to clarify that the displayed phase diagram concerns typical infinite-temperature energy density states in the thermodynamic limit.
examples.engagement_reason: He notes that very low-energy tail states would be localized and exponentially rare at infinite temperature, but does not analyze those tails concretely.

passes.reason: spectral-tail low-energy states sit at an energy extreme and refute an all-energies thermalization reading
The low-energy density-of-states tails are spectral-edge cases used to qualify/refute an all-energies reading of the infinite-temperature thermal phase statement.
Spectral-tail low-energy states are boundary-energy cases used to show an all-energies reading of the infinite-temperature phase diagram would fail.
the low-energy density-of-states tails are boundary-energy states used to qualify/refute an all-energies reading of the infinite-temperature ergodic statement
The spectral-tail low-energy states sit at an energy extreme and show that an all-energies thermal statement would fail.
The low-energy spectral-tail states lie at the edge of the energy distribution and are invoked to show an all-energies thermal statement would fail.
spectral-tail low-energy states are used to rule out an all-energies reading of the infinite-temperature claim.
The low-energy spectral-tail states are invoked as localized exceptions to any all-energies reading of the infinite-temperature thermal phase.
Spectral-tail low-energy states are boundary cases used to qualify the infinite-temperature ergodic statement.
low-energy density-of-states tails are spectral edge cases used to qualify/refute an all-energies ergodicity reading.
Low-energy tail states are invoked to rule out an all-energies reading, and they lie at an extremal spectral/density-of-states tail.
low-energy spectral-tail states are a caveat to any all-energy thermal/ergodic claim

examples.name: Temperature-gradient test for dc heat transport
examples.note: A hypothetical temperature gradient illustrates the diagnostic difference between the thermal phase, where heat flows, and the localized phase, where the gradient persists.
examples.engagement_reason: He uses a temperature-gradient setup as a diagnostic, reasoning that in the localized phase the gradient produces no steady heat flow and persists forever.

passes.reason: a temperature gradient is a standard transport diagnostic used to illustrate no dc heat flow
A persistent temperature gradient with no heat flow is an ordinary transport test deployed to show dc heat transport fails in the localized phase.
A temperature gradient with no steady heat flow is used to exhibit failure of dc heat transport in the localized phase.
a temperature gradient in the localized phase is used to show the failure of dc heat conduction despite the usual transport setup
An ordinary temperature-gradient setup is used to show dc heat flow/Fourier transport fails in the localized phase.
A persistent temperature gradient in the localized phase is used as a diagnostic showing dc heat conduction fails.
a persistent temperature gradient in the localized phase obstructs the usual expectation of dc/Fourier heat flow.
A maintained temperature gradient with no heat flow is used to exhibit the failure of dc heat transport in the localized phase.
A temperature gradient with no steady heat current is used to show failure of dc heat transport in the localized phase.
an ordinary temperature-gradient probe is used to show dc heat flow/conduction fails in the localized phase.
A temperature-gradient setup is a standard diagnostic for dc heat transport, used illustratively rather than as a counterexample.
a persistent temperature gradient is used to show dc heat conduction fails in the localized phase

examples.name: Writing a qubit on one spin
examples.note: The fate of quantum information written locally is used to illustrate decoherence in the thermal phase and persistent local coherence in the localized limit.
examples.engagement_reason: He explicitly traces what happens when quantum information is written on a spin/qubit, including Larmor precession at zero coupling and the localized eigenstate construction that keeps the qubit near a site.

passes.reason: a local spin qubit is an ordinary two-level subsystem used to illustrate coherence storage
A qubit written on a single spin is a minimal two-level encoding used to show local coherence need not decohere or spread in the localized phase.
A local spin qubit retaining coherence is used to show the usual decoherence/heat-bath expectation can fail.
writing a qubit on one spin is an ordinary local two-level storage construction used illustratively rather than to refute a claim
An ordinary local spin qubit is used to show the heat-bath/decoherence expectation can fail under many-body localization.
A local spin qubit is an ordinary two-level degree of freedom used to exhibit failure of decoherence/thermalization in the localized phase.
the one-spin qubit is a minimal local two-level encoding used to show thermal decoherence can fail in the localized phase.
Writing information on one spin is used as a test showing local coherence fails in the thermal phase and, conversely, need not decohere in the localized phase.
A local spin qubit is used to show that expected decoherence or thermalization can fail in the localized phase.
a standard single-spin qubit encoding is used to show local decoherence/thermalization can fail in the localized phase.
Writing a qubit on one spin is a standard local-coherence test in the spin chain, not a structurally special object or refutation.
a local qubit retaining coherence is used to show heat-bath decoherence can fail

examples.name: Single-particle Anderson transition and Thouless energy
examples.note: The known criterion that the Thouless energy matches the single-particle level spacing at criticality is used as the source of the analogous many-body scaling conjecture.
examples.engagement_reason: He works through the Thouless-energy picture for the single-particle Anderson transition, deriving the finite-size level spacing scaling and comparing it with diffusion and the many-body case.

passes.reason: the Anderson transition is a critical boundary where Thouless energy matches level spacing
The Anderson transition with Thouless energy is a critical boundary case used as an analogy for scaling, not as a refutation.
The Anderson transition is a critical boundary case where the Thouless energy matches the level spacing, used as an analogy rather than a refutation.
the single-particle Anderson transition is a critical boundary case where the Thouless energy becomes comparable to the level spacing, used as an analogy for the many-body transition
The Anderson transition is a critical boundary where the Thouless energy matches the level spacing, used as an analogy rather than a refutation.
The single-particle Anderson transition is a critical boundary case where the Thouless energy is compared with the level spacing.
the single-particle Anderson transition is a critical boundary case used by analogy via the Thouless-energy criterion.

The Anderson critical point and Thouless-energy criterion are boundary/critical cases used as an analogy for scaling, not as a refutation.
the Anderson critical point, where Thouless energy matches level spacing, is a boundary case used as an analogy.
The single-particle Anderson transition is used as an analogy, and the transition/Thouless-energy scaling is a critical boundary case.
